key_departures.impact: Phillips was acquired from Miami at the 2025 trade deadline for a 3rd-round pick and immediately became the Eagles' top pass rusher, leading the team with 34 pressures in 8 games (78% snap share). He signed a 4-year, $120M deal ($80M guaranteed) with Carolina — a contract the Eagles could not and would not match given future extension commitments to Mitchell, DeJean, and Carter. Leaves a massive void at EDGE with only Nolan Smith and Jalyx Hunt under contract at the position.

Dean signed a 3-year, $36M deal ($20M guaranteed) with the Raiders. He was the starting MIKE alongside Zack Baun and posted career-high production in 2024. However, the Eagles have Jihaad Campbell waiting in the wings — who was their 2025 first-round pick — plus Zack Baun (extended) and depth pieces Jeremiah Trotter Jr. and Smael Mondon. The interior LB corps behind Baun is thin but Campbell mitigates urgency.

Blankenship, an UDFA success story, was a full-time starter who captained the defense and started 50 games in 4 years in Philadelphia. He signed a 3-year, $24.75M deal with Houston. His departure leaves a genuine void at starting safety — the Eagles have Marcus Epps (also a free agent) and Andrew Mukuba (missed time with ankle fracture), plus Michael Carter II can flex to safety in a pinch, but no proven starter remains.

Goedert is the marquee offensive free agent still unsigned as of March 10. He set a franchise TE TD record with 11 touchdowns in 2025 and is the clear starter. Reports from the NFL Combine indicated he would not return. Eagles have interest but reportedly cannot afford his asking price. Grant Calcaterra and Kylen Granson (both UFA) also departing, leaving the TE room entirely depleted. This is arguably the most glaring offensive hole entering the draft.

Dotson signed a 2-year, $15M deal with Atlanta ($10M guaranteed). He was a WR3/4 in Philadelphia who never fully justified the trade capital used to acquire him. Departure is minimal impact — DeVonta Smith and AJ Brown (pending trade) remain, and the Eagles have Quez Watkins and Danny Gray on futures deals.

Calcaterra was Goedert's primary backup with blocking deficiencies. His departure alongside Goedert and Kylen Granson empties the TE room. Compounds the TE need created by Goedert's expected departure.
Granson was a one-year signing who contributed mostly on special teams (7 catches, 40 yards). Minimal production loss but adds to the complete void at the TE position.
Epps was a backup/rotational safety. His departure alongside Blankenship further thins a safety room that is now near-empty at the starter level.
Uche was a rotational rusher who produced 1 sack and 15 pressures in 12 games. His departure further thins an EDGE corps that already lost Phillips. Adds to the escalating EDGE need.

Graham came out of retirement mid-2025 to help the Eagles. At 38, likely heading back to retirement. His departure is symbolic — he was never a full-season contributor — but thins the EDGE room further.

Jackson actually re-signed with the Eagles on a one-year deal after being listed as a departing free agent. He provides veteran CB depth behind Mitchell and DeJean. NOTE: Treated as a re-signing/addition below.

signings.analysis: Davis's extension is the Eagles' most important offseason move. The 3-year, $78M deal ($65M fully guaranteed) makes him the highest-paid nose tackle in NFL history and locks him in through 2029 alongside Jalen Carter (pending extension/trade decision). Davis broke out in 2025 with 4.5 sacks, 72 tackles, and a blocked FG TD. This extension cements the interior DL as a non-need — Carter's situation (trade calls active, fifth-year option decision by May 1) is the only wrinkle. If Carter remains, IDL is a premium strength. Even in a trade scenario, Davis and Moro Ojomo form a capable 1-2.

Jackson re-signed on a near-minimum 1-year deal after the Eagles listed him as a free agent. He provides veteran depth and competition behind All-Pro starters Quinyon Mitchell and Cooper DeJean, both of whom are locked in as starters. At $1.755M, this is purely a depth/competition move — Jackson's best days are behind him. CB is not a current need with Mitchell and DeJean, so this is maintenance. CB need stays unchanged (non-need at starter level, modest depth concern only).

The Eagles renegotiated Carter's contract specifically to reduce his $10.1M cap hit and create space. Carter played just 41 defensive snaps after arriving from the Jets at the 2025 deadline. He provides depth/insurance at CB and can flex to safety — meaningful given Blankenship's departure. This is a cap management move first, a roster move second. Exact new terms not yet available. Keeps a useful DB on the roster without the full cap burden. Contributes modestly to the S depth hole but doesn't solve it.

Graham was re-signed on March 2 from the practice squad to an offseason/reserve contract. He never appeared in a game for the Eagles in 2025. A camp body with 51 career games of experience from Atlanta. This is a depth signing with no material impact on needs — IDL is already addressed via Davis's extension. He'll compete for a roster spot in training camp.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Phillips (4yr/$120M, $80M gtd) signed with Carolina, Uche departed, and Graham likely retiring. Only Nolan Smith and Jalyx Hunt remain under contract from last year's active roster. Smith is developmental; Hunt showed promise but neither is a proven starter. Eagles have zero signed EDGEs at starter-quality money. This is now the clearest, most urgent need on the roster. Roseman reportedly explored a Jonathan Greenard trade. Premium pick (Rd 1-2) or top-tier FA signing essential.

Dallas Goedert (franchise record 11 TDs in 2025) is expected to leave — he officially made the open market and is unsigned. Calcaterra and Granson also departed. The TE room is entirely empty of proven players. With Jalen Hurts needing a reliable target and a new OC (Sean Mannion) installing his system, TE is tied with EDGE as the most critical need. Eagles were reportedly interested in David Njoku. A round 1-3 TE pick or veteran signing is imperative.

Blankenship (3yr/$24.75M) signed with Houston; Epps also departed. Andrew Mukuba (ankle fracture late 2025) is the best in-house option but unproven as a full-time starter. Michael Carter II can flex there in a pinch but is not a safety by trade. The Eagles need to add a starting-caliber safety — whether through mid-FA, trade, or the draft. Should target rounds 2-4 or a mid-tier veteran bridge.

Dean (3yr/$36M) signed with Las Vegas, but the need does NOT escalate dramatically because Jihaad Campbell (2025 first-round pick) is waiting in the wings. Zack Baun (extended) and Campbell form a capable starting tandem. Jeremiah Trotter Jr. and Smael Mondon provide depth. LB need remains moderate/unchanged — they may add a veteran for insurance but it's not a premium draft investment.

Jordan Davis's 3yr/$78M extension ($65M gtd) locks him in as one of the best interior DL in football through 2029. Jalen Carter (pending fifth-year option and/or extension/trade) remains elite when healthy. Moro Ojomo provides rotational depth. IDL drops from a need to a luxury — only a developmental/comp pick is warranted. The one caveat: if Carter is traded, IDL becomes a tier 2 need immediately.

Quinyon Mitchell and Cooper DeJean both earned First Team All-Pro in 2025 and are locked in as top-tier starters (extensions expected in 2027). Adoree' Jackson re-signed on a minimum deal, and Michael Carter II was retained. The CB need is minimal — depth is thin but the starting group is elite. Low-priority draft pick (Rd 5-7) or UDFA to add competition.

AJ Brown's trade status is the defining unknown. As of March 10, he has NOT been traded — he remains on the roster with a $23.4M cap hit. If Brown stays, WR is not a need with Smith and Brown. If traded (asking price is reportedly 1st + 2nd), WR escalates to tier 1 immediately. Dotson's loss at WR3 is minor. This tier is contingent on the Brown situation resolving. Flag as VOLATILE — reassess upon trade resolution.

key_additions.headline: Record-breaking NT extension ($65M guaranteed) locks down the IDL through 2029 after a breakout 2025 season
Veteran CB depth signing on near-minimum deal; reinforces the room behind All-Pro starters Mitchell and DeJean
Cap-friendly renegotiation retains versatile DB who can play nickel and emergency safety
Veteran DL depth re-sign from practice squad; camp competition only

key_losses.headline: Eagles' top pass rusher (34 pressures in 8 games) departs on massive $120M deal; EDGE is now the most critical need
Franchise-record 11-TD tight end reaches free market; his departure empties the entire TE room
UDFA success story and 50-game starter departs; safety is now a clear hole with Mukuba unproven
Starting MIKE departs but Jihaad Campbell's emergence softens the blow
WR3 trade acquisition departs; minimal impact on depth chart unless AJ Brown is also traded

notes.aj_brown_watch: AJ Brown has NOT been traded as of March 10. The Eagles' asking price is reportedly a 1st + 2nd round pick. Trade talks with the Patriots stalled. If Brown is traded, WR escalates to tier 1 need and the team's entire draft strategy shifts. This is the most volatile roster variable heading into the draft.
notes.jalen_carter_watch: The Eagles received trade calls for Carter from multiple teams (Packers, Bears, 49ers, Seahawks, Raiders per reports). The fifth-year option ($27.13M) must be decided by May 1. If extended or option exercised, IDL remains a non-need. If traded, IDL jumps to tier 2 and EDGE becomes even more critical.
notes.compensatory_picks: Eagles received 4 compensatory picks in 2026 (3rd/98, 4th/137, 5th/178, 6th/215) for 2025 FA departures (Becton, Rodgers, Sweat, Milton Williams). Total of 9 picks entering the draft. Roseman is reportedly being cautious about signing outside FAs to preserve potential 2027 compensatory picks.
notes.oline_stability: Lane Johnson confirmed returning for Year 14. Landon Dickerson expected back. OL coach Chris Kuper installed. The OL need is minimal — depth could be addressed in mid-rounds.
